manual_guardrails: Do not caricature Pharisees, scribes, or Jewish people; the custom explanation is context, not collective blame.
Do not portray Jesus as racist or cruel, or the Syrophoenician mother as manipulative; be honest that his full intent is partly unknown.
Never generalize illness, disability, or mental-health conditions as demonic, and do not make persistence a miracle formula.
Treat the deaf man with dignity; no invented diagnosis, no promise that faith always heals disability.
Show the debated verse-31 route as a broad possible route, never a false precise line.
textual_variants: Mark 7:16 is omitted from the ESV main text (later-manuscript addition; do not invent it and keep ranges spanning it contiguous).
Mark 7:4 washing terms vary across manuscripts and editions; explain without overclaiming.

locations.name: Gennesaret
locations.display: Known plain on the NW shore of the Sea of Galilee (the crowd of 6:53–7:1 carries in); shown as an orientation point.

locations.name: Tyre
locations.display: Jesus withdraws to the region of Tyre (7:24). The city is a known site and is shown for orientation; the house itself is unrecorded and never pinned.

locations.name: Sidon
locations.display: Named on the return route (7:31), a known coastal city north of Tyre; shown for orientation.

locations.name: Decapolis
locations.display: The deaf man is healed 'in the region of the Decapolis' (7:31) — a real, named league of cities SE of the Sea of Galilee. The region is shown with an approximate boundary; the healing spot itself is not given and gets no pin.

locations.name: Route Tyre to Sidon to Decapolis
locations.display: The journey of 7:31 runs Tyre, north to Sidon, then down to the Decapolis — a famously roundabout way. The order of places comes from the text; the exact road is unrecorded, so a broad sweep is shown, never a precise line.

manual_guardrails: Do not state that the feeding crowd was entirely Gentile.
Do not portray Pharisees as priests, foreigners, or visual villains.
Do not make the Pan grotto, a temple, or Mount Hermon the stated confession site.
Do not describe Peter as demon-possessed.
Do not use cross-bearing to sanctify abuse, coercion, burnout, self-harm, or preventable danger.

locations.name: Dalmanutha
locations.display: 'The district of Dalmanutha' (8:10) has never been securely identified — no site is agreed. No pin; the uncertainty is stated plainly (some manuscripts read Magadan/Magdala, itself debated).

locations.name: Bethsaida
locations.display: The blind man is healed at Bethsaida (8:22). Mark names the town plainly, but its modern identification is disputed between two nearby candidate sites (et-Tell and el-Araj) — an area covering both is shown, not a single pin.

locations.name: Caesarea Philippi
locations.display: Peter's confession comes 'on the way to the villages of Caesarea Philippi' (8:27). The city at the foot of Mount Hermon is a known site, shown for orientation; the confession happened in the surrounding villages, so there is no event pin.

locations.name: Feeding of the 4,000
locations.display: The feeding (8:1–9) is on the Decapolis / SE side of the lake — a probable broader area, no exact spot and no pin.

required_movements.name: The hinge saying
required_movements.reason: Verse 1 bridges 8:38 and the mountain: some standing here will see the kingdom having come with power.

required_movements.name: The Transfiguration
required_movements.reason: Jesus transfigured with Moses and Elijah; the Father's voice — 'listen to him' — closes the scene.

required_movements.name: Coming down: the Elijah question
required_movements.reason: Silence until the rising, and the disciples' Elijah question answered on the descent.

required_movements.name: The suffering boy and his father
required_movements.reason: The failed disciples, the father's honest 'I believe; help my unbelief,' and prayer-dependence belong to one scene.

required_movements.name: The second passion prediction
required_movements.reason: A private teaching passage: delivered, killed, rising — and they did not understand.

required_movements.name: Greatness and the child
required_movements.reason: The Capernaum argument about who is greatest is answered with a servant's place and a child.

required_movements.name: The outsider in Jesus' name
required_movements.reason: John's gatekeeping is corrected: whoever is not against us is for us.

required_movements.name: Stumbling, salt, and peace
required_movements.reason: The severe hyperbole against causing stumbling runs into salt and peace as one closing charge.

manual_guardrails: Do not identify an exact Transfiguration mountain as fact.
Do not diagnose the boy or generalize seizures, disability, or mental illness as demonic.
Do not blame the boy or father, displace medical care, or promise the same outcome to every family.
Do not literalize the hand, foot, and eye sayings as self-harm instructions.
Do not call Gehenna a perpetually burning first-century municipal dump.
textual_variants: Mark 9:44 and 9:46: later-manuscript additions affect numbering in some editions.
Mark 9:49: wording and interpretation require edition-aware review.

locations.name: Capernaum
locations.display: Known site on the north shore of the Sea of Galilee (9:33); shown as a point.

locations.name: High mountain of the Transfiguration
locations.display: Mark leaves the high mountain unnamed (9:2). Tabor is traditional and a Hermon-area setting is argued from the preceding chapter's geography — no pin; the uncertainty is stated plainly.

locations.name: Passage through Galilee
locations.display: They passed through Galilee (9:30) by an unrecorded route — no pin and no invented line.

manual_guardrails: Use wealthy man for Mark-local copy; young comes from Matthew and ruler from Luke.
Do not invent a Needle Gate workaround.
Do not use the divorce teaching to trap someone in danger or force unsafe reunion.
Do not invent the wealthy man's motives, later fate, office, or age.
Do not portray Bartimaeus through fabricated diagnosis or disability spectacle.
Do not present palms, a whip, the Court of the Gentiles, or dishonest exchange rates as Mark-explicit details.
Do not turn the fig-tree and Temple interpretation into a curse on Jewish people or ethnic Israel.
Do not portray Jewish leaders, merchants, or crowds as racially distinct villains.
Do not turn prayer into a prosperity guarantee or blame unanswered prayer on weak faith.
Do not define forgiveness as unsafe reunion, automatic trust, or absence of accountability.
textual_variants: Mark 11:26: important earlier witnesses omit the added sentence found in later manuscripts.
